title: Data Modeling
description: Design MongoDB schemas — when to embed documents, when to use references, and real-world design patterns.

sections.content: The biggest mental shift from SQL: MongoDB gives you a choice. You can **embed** related data inside the same document (denormalization), or **reference** it with an ID (like a foreign key). The right choice depends on how you query the data.

sections.heading: Example: Embed vs Reference for author data

sections.content: **The golden rule:** Model your data for the most common query, not for theoretical purity. If you always fetch a book with its author, embed. If you often update author profiles, reference.

sections.heading: One-to-One: Embed in most cases
One-to-Many: Embed when the 'many' side is small and bounded
Many-to-Many: Reference with an array of IDs

sections.content: **Avoid unbounded arrays:** Never embed a list that could grow very large (orders, comments, log entries). MongoDB has a 16MB document size limit, and large embedded arrays cause performance issues. Use a separate collection + reference instead.
MongoDB's flexibility means the same data can be modelled many ways. These named patterns are solutions to common problems — learn them so you can recognise and apply them.
**Extended Reference Pattern** — store a foreign key AND a few frequently-needed fields from the referenced document to avoid a `$lookup` on every read.

sections.heading: Extended Reference — cache key fields to avoid $lookup

sections.content: **Subset Pattern** — for large arrays (like product reviews), embed only the most recently accessed subset inside the parent document. Store the full list in a separate collection.

sections.heading: Subset Pattern — hot data embedded, cold data referenced

sections.content: **Bucket Pattern** — instead of one document per event (millions of tiny documents) or all events in one document (exceeds 16MB), group N events into one 'bucket' document.

sections.heading: Bucket Pattern — group sensor readings by hour

sections.content: **Outlier Pattern** — most documents fit a normal pattern, but a few are exceptions. Add an overflow flag to outliers and handle them separately in code.

sections.heading: Outlier Pattern — handle exceptions differently

sections.content: **Schema Versioning Pattern** — when your schema evolves, not all existing documents update instantly. Add a `schemaVersion` field so your application knows how to handle each document.

sections.heading: Schema Versioning — handle multiple document shapes

sections.content: **The golden rule of MongoDB schema design: model for your access patterns, not for theoretical correctness.** Ask 'what queries will I run?' before deciding embed vs reference. In SQL you normalise first then optimise later. In MongoDB, you design for your queries upfront.

**Tree Hierarchies** (categories, org charts) have multiple patterns. The right choice depends on how you query the tree: **Adjacency list** (store just the `parentId`) is simple for parent-child lookups. **Materialized path** (store the full path string like `/root/parent/child`) makes subtree queries easy with regex. **Array of ancestors** stores all parent IDs in an array for fast ancestor lookups.
